Name: Pattern Recognition - IND
Text: Intended flow R A E P N C Model constraints init R starts Matches R first existence C must exist Matches C end responded existence E if A Matches A E precedence R before C Matches noncoexistence E and C cannot occur together

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Anomaly 1 noncoexistence between E and C Intended flow requires E before C If E and C noncoexistent claim cannot be closed if evaluated But existence says C must happen So if C happens E cannot happen But responded existence says if A happens E must happen So if A happens E happens If E happens C cannot happen But C must happen This creates contradiction If A happens process cannot complete noncoexistence major blocker forbids valid path R A E C

Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Anomaly 2 Missing constraints No constraint forces A E P N to exist No constraint enforces order between A E P N Only R and C constrained relative This allows paths like R C skips everything undermines business logic of evaluation and approval

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Anomaly 3 responded existence E if A combined with noncoexistence E C means A E No C Since C existence A Failure to close model forbids assigning adjuster if claim must be closed

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Why noncoexistence E C Maybe confusion between Close and Close without evaluation Maybe E stands for something else Maybe data error E and C never seen together Maybe C means Cancel E implies valid so dont coexist Technical error in mining tool Why missing constraints Model incomplete Mining parameters loose Focus only start end

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Check for claims with C but no E Actually if model says E and C dont coexist check if real data has E and C together Query Find claims with both E and C If returns rows noncoexistence violated

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Propose SQL queries Check cooccurrence E and C SELECT claim_id FROM claim_events WHERE activity IN E C GROUP BY claim_id HAVING COUNT DISTINCT activity 2 Check A and C SELECT claim_id FROM claim_events WHERE activity IN A C GROUP BY claim_id HAVING COUNT DISTINCT activity 2 Check skipped steps R and C no A E P N Check claims with A but no C Check adjuster assignment resource matching adjusters
